text: This video highlights the importance of using a circular anal dilator to both diagnose and also guide fixation of internal rectal prolapse during surgery, getting it right first time. Internal rectal prolapse is a full thickness interception of the rectum. Whilst it can occur in normal individuals, it has been shown to be morphologically more advanced in symptomatic patients with more high-grade or rectoanal interception present.

Patients are usually seen in the pelvic floor clinic and then we'll have specialist investigations. While some centres use conventional x-ray dynamic proctography, we use MRI, which provides higher quality images of the pelvic viscera and floor. This sequence of MRI images shows a patient with ODS due to a high-grade rectoanal interception. MRI also allows accurate assessment of all three pelvic floor compartments.
However, whilst providing accurate images, MRI can both understage and also fail to diagnose rectal interception. We have found this in our own unit.

A clear circular anal dilator, such as the one shown here, is a very accurate way of demonstrating an internal rectal prolapse and also assessing its grade. We use the Oxford grade. It overcomes the limitations of proctography and allows an anterior or posterior predominance to be noted. As well as diagnosing IRP, we advocate the use of the CAD during corrective surgery to ensure that the IRP has been fully reduced prior to fixation.
This first video highlights the importance of using the CAD at the time of surgery to ensure the rectum is adequately elevated out of the pelvis to reduce the prolapse.

The patient is a sixty-year-old lady with severe ODS, but fortunately without concomitant faecal incontinence. Her risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse include three previous vaginal deliveries and also a hysterectomy. MRDP, whilst confirming full thickness rectal prolapse, showed this only to be intra-rectal. MRI also identified pelvic floor descent and erectocele. A transit study had shown no evidence of slave transit.
Treatment was initiated with correct positioning during defecation, laxatives and pelvic floor physiotherapy. Despite these, her symptoms persisted and the patient was requesting surgery.

At EUA, a grade three IRP was found and due to significant sigmoid pathology, an attempt at VMR was abandoned. She was subsequently rediscussed and offered resection rectopexy. A second EUA had identified progression of the degree of IRP, which was now grade four posteriorly.

During the laparoscopic surgery, the surgeon fully mobilizes the sigmoid colon and then mobilizes the rectum down to the pelvic floor. Following the resection of the sigmoid colon, a stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed, which is confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused through endoscopic visualization.

Three months post-op, the patient reports an improved stool frequency and her ODS symptoms have dramatically improved. She is very satisfied so far with the outcome of her surgery, but longer term data is awaited.

In our second patient, we again demonstrate the value of the CAD to help guide correction of prolapse. This lady is a seventy-three-year-old patient with long-standing ADS who has recently started to experience faecal incontinence. She had significant risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse and pelvic floor weakness. A full pelvic floor workup confirmed high-grade interception on MRDP with normal sphincter pressures at manometry and anatomically intact sphincters on endo-anal ultrasound. Interestingly enough, there was a thickened sphincter, which is seen in IRP.

Optimal medical management was instigated, but despite this, at six months' follow-up, she was no better clinically. Discussion at the pelvic floor MDT led to a recommendation for a laparoscopic ventral motor ectopexy to correct the IRP.

During the examination under anesthesia (EUA) at the time of surgery, an Oxford Grade IV internal rectal prolapse is confirmed. Laparoscopy reveals severe diverticular disease with the sigmoid colon adherent to the peritoneum over the vaginal vault, leading to the abandonment of the surgery for further discussion.

The surgeon frees the sigmoid colon from the vaginal vault, revealing a deep and wide pelvis. After dividing the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), the rectum is mobilized in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane down to the pelvic floor. The sigmoid colon is resected, and a stapled side-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused.
When reviewed at twelve months post-surgery the patient was considerably better with no further incontinence episodes and resolution of her ODS symptoms. Delighted with her outcome.

In conclusion, we believe that the management for all patients with internal rectal prolapse ought to always commence conservatively using the salt to cooking mantra. In cases that fail, we advocate discussion of pelvic floor MDT and the use of a circular anal dilator in cases where surgery is considered to ensure complete reduction of the prolapse. Thank you for listening.
